nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef flats, lagoons, and reef slopes; commonly found on dead coral rubble, hard substrate, and mixed-sediment zones

decorPreferences: open placement away from other corals
avoid placement near corals you don't want overgrown

diet.notes: Fully photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae; does not require target feeding. The distinctive pulsing motion of the polyps appears to aid CO2 uptake for photosynthesis, substantially boosting photosynthetic efficiency. No supplemental feeding of plankton or nutrients is generally needed.

goodWith: other soft corals with adequate spacing
LPS corals if kept at a distance
avoidWith: adjacent corals of any kind without containment barriers
SPS corals in close proximity — Xenia spreading can smother them
other Xeniidae species if tank space is limited

summary: Xenia is a genus of soft corals famed for their rhythmic polyp pulsing, which aids photosynthesis. Among the easiest corals to keep but notoriously unpredictable — can thrive explosively or crash without warning.
careNotes:
Note: Xenia is a genus (~54 described species) sold under various trade names. The most common aquarium species are X. umbellata and related classic Xenia (true 'pulsing' types), while X. elongata has been reclassified as Unomia stolonifera; both share near-identical husbandry requirements in the hobby. The scientificName field is set to 'Xenia sp.' to reflect this genus-level trade convention.

Xenia has a complicated reputation in reef keeping. Many experienced aquarists regard it as simultaneously the easiest soft coral and one of the most frustrating. Key characteristics:

• Pulsing behavior: The distinctive rhythmic pulsing of the polyps is driven by photosynthetic need — research has shown pulsing draws CO2-rich water toward the zooxanthellae, enhancing photosynthesis by an order of magnitude compared to resting. Pulsing may slow or stop completely if pH drops below approximately 8.3, under low-intensity lighting, or with excessively strong or laminar flow. Cessation of pulsing is not necessarily a sign of imminent decline — colonies have been documented continuing to reproduce even while not pulsing.

• Unpredictability: Xenia is well-documented as both 'the easiest soft coral' and 'frustratingly unpredictable.' Established colonies have been known to crash suddenly and completely without any apparent trigger or change in water parameters. Conversely, a fragment placed in the right tank tends to spread rapidly. This boom-or-bust pattern is poorly understood and widely discussed among hobbyists.

• Spreading: Under favorable conditions, Xenia tends to spread aggressively by sending runners along rockwork, glass, and substrate, potentially overgrowing neighboring corals within weeks to months. Colonies have been observed dominating 80%+ of reef surfaces in field studies. In the aquarium, periodic pruning with scissors is commonly needed. Placing Xenia on isolated frag plugs, islands, or easily-removable rubble allows controlled removal. Not recommended as a first coral in a tank with prized SPS colonies.

• Flow: Low-to-moderate, non-laminar (random/tumbling) flow is generally preferred. Excessive direct flow tends to stop pulsing and can stress the colony. Single small powerhead or gentle gyre-type flow is typically adequate.

• Lighting: Tolerates a wide range of intensities (approximately 50–150 PAR). Tends to grow faster under brighter light but can thrive under modest reef lighting. No spectrum preference documented.

• Water quality: Generally tolerant of less-than-pristine water conditions compared to SPS corals, but pH stability above 8.1–8.3 appears important for maintaining pulsing behavior. No iodine supplementation required.

• Reef safe status: Generally considered reef safe in the sense that it does not sting neighboring corals. However, its aggressive spreading behavior can smother slower-growing neighbors, and its rapid encrustation of rockwork and substrate is the main compatibility concern.

• Subspecies note: Varieties marketed as 'Silver Xenia,' 'Blue Xenia,' 'Pom-Pom Xenia,' and others are color morphs of the same genus. WWC has documented a 'True Blue Xenia' that does not pulse but displays striking blue coloration.
breedingNotes: Xenia reproduces rapidly in captivity both asexually (by fragmentation and runner/stolon extension) and sexually. Fragging is simple: cut a polyp cluster with scissors or a blade and attach to rubble or a frag plug with reef-safe gel. Colonies can detach naturally and reattach elsewhere in the tank. Fragged colonies recover quickly under good conditions. Sexual reproduction via broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs but is not deliberately practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Soft Corals & Polyps (category, Xenia not currently listed separately)
primary.notes: LiveAquaria's Xenia product page was not accessible during research (no dedicated species page found in catalog at time of access). Care parameters sourced from secondary sources; LiveAquaria used as primary source reference per slice definition.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Why Did My Pulse Corals Stop Pulsing?
additional.notes: Key husbandry source: pH threshold for pulsing (8.3), flow preferences (low-moderate, non-laminar), photosynthetic nutrition, colony crash behavior, 23-year captive colony notes. Cross-verified water parameters and flow.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Pulsing Xenia Mystery Solved
additional.notes: Scientific research on pulsing mechanism (CO2/RuBisCo photosynthesis aid). Confirms photosynthetic, no plankton feeding needed. Oxygen concentration affects pulsing cessation.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Unomia is the New Name of Xenia elongata
additional.notes: Taxonomic note: X. elongata reclassified as Unomia stolonifera. Confirms extreme growth rate ('xenia reactors'). Family Xeniidae confirmed.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Xenia (genus)
additional.notes: Taxonomy: Order Malacalcyonacea, Family Xeniidae (cross-confirmed via NCBI). 54 recognized species. X. umbellata and X. elongata noted. Indo-Pacific and Red Sea range. Pulsing behavior described.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Show Soft Corals Some Love (shallow lagoon tank)
additional.notes: Practical tank observations: thrived in 200L shallow lagoon with minimal dosing, no supplemental feeding needed, requires regular pruning. Called 'Antichrist among most experienced reefers' for spreading. Confirmed low-moderate flow tolerance.
